title: Floating point numbers should not be tested for equality
description: Floating point numbers in C# (and in most other programming languages) are not precise. They are a binary approximation of the actual value. This means that even if two floating point numbers appear to be equal, they might not be due to the tiny differences in their binary representation.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Detects direct equality (==) or inequality (!=) comparisons on floating point types (float, double, decimal)
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when: method or property source contains == or != operators with floating point operands on the same line
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes: epsilon-based comparisons (Math.Abs on same line), logging statements (Console/Logger on same line as comparison), comments
never_trigger_on: Logging statements where comparison is inside Console.WriteLine
String formatting for display where comparison is in interpolation
logic.reasoning: Floating point numbers are imprecise due to binary representation, making direct equality checks unreliable. This rule uses a line-by-line approach to detect == or != operators applied to floating point literals (0.1f, 0.2f) or declarations. The pattern matches lines containing float/double declarations or literals followed by equality operators. Excludes lines with Math.Abs (epsilon-based comparison), and avoids false positives from logging by requiring no Console/Logger on the same line as the comparison.

should_trigger: if (sum == g) // where sum and g are floats
